context_after: -- | @id x@ and @x@ are the same value, so an 'ElideCall' mutant here would be
-- equivalent and unkillable.  Calls to 'id' are skipped by default, so
context_before: -- with @succ x@) and @succ x@ (replaced with @x@), since each has an 'Int'
-- argument and an 'Int' result.  The test distinguishes all three values.
mitigation: If `succ` is an identity on this argument this is an equivalent mutant that no test can kill; add `succ` to this operator's `skip-calls-to` config to suppress it.

context_after: -- | @id x@ and @x@ are the same value, so an 'ElideCall' mutant here would be
-- equivalent and unkillable.  Calls to 'id' are skipped by default, so
context_before: -- with @succ x@) and @succ x@ (replaced with @x@), since each has an 'Int'
-- argument and an 'Int' result.  The test distinguishes all three values.
mitigation: If `abs` is an identity on this argument this is an equivalent mutant that no test can kill; add `abs` to this operator's `skip-calls-to` config to suppress it.

context_after: -- | A nested application.  'ElideCall' fires on both @abs (succ x)@ (replaced
-- with @succ x@) and @succ x@ (replaced with @x@), since each has an 'Int'
context_before: -- | 'negate' on an 'Int' returns an 'Int', so 'ElideCall' replaces @negate x@
-- with @x@.  The two differ for any non-zero input, so the test kills it.
mitigation: If `negate` is an identity on this argument this is an equivalent mutant that no test can kill; add `negate` to this operator's `skip-calls-to` config to suppress it.
